10.1016/j.jmp.2016.04.013
Probability theory began in 1654 with the mathematicians Blaise Pascal and Pierre de Fermat providing advice to the gambler Chevalier de Mere. Inherent in gambling games is the premise that the outcome of the gamble will be completely determined at some future time to the satisfaction of the participants: that is, it will be determined that event on which the gamble is based has occurred or it will be determined that its complement has occurred. This imposes a condition called “the Law of the Excluded Middle” on the logical structure of the space of gambling events.  Boole (1854)  formulated a logical structure for gambling events that today is called “a boolean algebra of events”. One of its conditions is the Law of the Excluded Middle. There are kinds of gambling situations for which the Law of the Excluded Middle fails.

One example is betting on the truths of arithmetical statements. It is well-known that there is no universal method of determining which statements of an explicit formulation of arithmetic are true and which are false, thus producing a failure of the Law of the Excluded Middle (from the epistemic perspective of the betters, since they do not expect all of the statements they are betting on to have determinate truth values). “Intuitionistic logic” is a non-boolean logic formulated by  Heyting (1930)  to deal with this kind of “incompleteness”. It produces a more general event algebra than a boolean algebra of events, and its probabilistic use requires a more generalized form of a probability function than the one traditionally employed for a boolean algebra of events.

Another kind of probabilistic event algebra appeared in the early formulation of quantum mechanics ( Dirac, 1930; von Neumann, 1932 ). It also was a generalization of a boolean algebra of events, and it required a corresponding generalization of the notion of probability function. The quantum version allows for events  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       such that the smallest event including them, denoted by  
                         A 
                         ⋓ 
                         B 
                      , is different from their set-theoretic union,  
                         A 
                         ∪ 
                         B 
                      .

Except for quantum mechanics, probability theories based on boolean algebra of events have come to dominate science and philosophy. This article investigates alternatives to the boolean theories. The development is done in a manner designed to provide insight into why the quantum and intuitionistic generalizations or their close algebraic relatives appear to be the few options for useful generalizations of “event space” and “probability function” for possible scientific inquiry and application.

This article is designed to be an introduction to lattice theory and its application to the mathematical and philosophical foundations of probability. Much of the article’s material is based on the development of  Narens (2015)  and the proofs of many of the presented theorems can be found in that work.

Formally, a  boolean algebra of events  is a set  
                         X 
                        of subsets of a nonempty set  
                         X 
                       such that  
                         
                            • 
                            
                               
                                  X 
                                and the empty set,  
                                  ∅ 
                               , are in  
                                  X 
                               , 
                         
                         
                            • 
                            
                               
                                  X 
                                  ≠ 
                                  ∅ 
                               , and 
                         
                         
                            • 
                            if  
                                  A 
                                and  
                                  B 
                                are in  
                                  X 
                               , then  
                                  A 
                                  ∪ 
                                  B 
                               ,  
                                  A 
                                  ∩ 
                                  B 
                               , and  
                                  − 
                                  A 
                                are in  
                                  X 
                               , where  
                                  ∪ 
                               ,  
                                  ∩ 
                               , and  
                                  − 
                                are, respectively, set-theoretic union, intersection, and complementation with respect to  
                                  X 
                               . 
                         
                       Elements of  
                         X 
                        are called  events ,  
                         X 
                       is called the  sure event,  and the empty set,  
                         ∅ 
                      , is called the  impossible  or  null event . Elements of  
                         X 
                       are interpreted as  possible states  of the world, or just  states  for short. Among the possible states of the world is the  true state , which is generally unknown. Associated with  
                         X 
                        is a  boolean probability function 
                      
                         P 
                      . For events  
                         A 
                       in  
                         X 
                      ,  
                         P 
                         
                            ( 
                            A 
                            ) 
                         
                       is interpreted as the degree of belief that the true state of the world is in  
                         A 
                      .  
                         P 
                        is assumed to have the following properties for all  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       in  
                         X 
                      :

                                  P

There are a number of ways to generalize boolean probability theory. In the literature, particularly the psychological literature, the generalizations usually take the form of assuming a non-finitely additive probability function on a boolean algebra of events. This article focuses on a different strategy for generalization: generalizing both the underlying boolean algebra of events and its probability function so that the probability function retains a useful form of finite additivity. This second kind of generalization is useful for understanding the impact of context on judgment and measurement—a ubiquitous and important issue throughout science.

In a boolean algebra of events  
                         X 
                      ,  
                         ∪ 
                       and  
                         ∩ 
                       can be characterized in terms of the set-theoretic subset relation,  
                         ⊆ 
                      , as follows: For all  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       in  
                         X 
                      ,  
                         
                            • 
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  ∪ 
                                  B 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     the 
                                  
                                  
                                  ⊆ 
                               -smallest set  
                                  C 
                                in  
                                  X 
                                 such that  
                                  A 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  C 
                                and  
                                  B 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  C 
                               , and 
                         
                         
                            • 
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  ∩ 
                                  B 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     the 
                                  
                                  
                                  ⊆ 
                               -largest set  
                                  D 
                                in  
                                  X 
                                 such that  
                                  D 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  A 
                                and  
                                  D 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  B 
                               . 
                         
                       The above definition of  
                         A 
                         ∪ 
                         B 
                       says that  
                         C 
                       is the  
                         ⊆ 
                      -least upper bound in  
                         X 
                        of  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       and that  
                         A 
                         ∩ 
                         B 
                       is the  
                         ⊆ 
                      -greatest lower bound of  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       in  
                         X 
                      . The suggests the following generalization of  
                         ∪ 
                       and  
                         ∩ 
                       and “boolean algebra of events”:

A 
                                  ∪ 
                                  B 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     the 
                                  
                                  
                                  ⊆ 
                               -smallest set  
                                  C 
                                in  
                                  X 
                                 such that  
                                  A 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  C 
                                and  
                                  B

A

                                in  
                                  X 
                                 such that  
                                  D 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  A 
                                and  
                                  D 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  B 
                               .

The  algebra  inherent in boolean algebra of events is captured by the following definition and theorem.

There are two notions of isomorphism that can be productively defined to hold between two lattice algebras of events

                         = 
                         
                            〈 
                            X 
                            , 
                            ⋓

                         =

                            ,

                            ∅ 
                            〉 
                         
                      . First,  
                         X 
                        and  
                         Y 
                        are said to be  lattice isomorphic  if and only if there exists a one-to-one function  
                         α 
                          from  
                      
                         X 
                          onto  
                      
                         Y 
                       such that for all  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       in  
                         X 
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               α 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               Y 
                               , 
                               
                               α 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  ∅ 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               ∅ 
                               , 
                               
                               α 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               α 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     ⋓ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               α 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               , 
                               
                               and 
                               
                               α 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               α 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     ⋒ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               α 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                       The second notion of isomorphism is a structure preserving function  
                         β 
                          from  
                      
                         X 
                       
                      onto  
                      
                         Y 
                      : For each  
                         Z 
                       in  
                         X 
                      , let  
                         
                            
                               β 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Z 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  { 
                                  β 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ∣ 
                                  x 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Z 
                                  } 
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                       Then  
                         β 
                        is said to be an  event isomorphism  from  
                         X 
                        onto  
                         Y 
                        if and only if  
                         β 
                        is a one-to-one function  from  
                      
                         X 
                       
                      onto  
                      
                         Y 
                       such that for all  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       in  
                         X 
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               β 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               β 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     ⋓ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               β 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               , 
                               
                               and 
                               
                               β 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               β 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     ⋒ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               β 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                       All event isomorphisms are lattice isomorphisms. An example is given below of a lattice isomorphism from a boolean lattice algebra of events onto a boolean algebra of events that is not an event isomorphism.

Theorem 1 
                         
                            Stone, 1936, 1937 
                         
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             
                            be a distributive lattice algebra and  
                            
                               Y 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  Y 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        ⋓ 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        ⋒ 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           – 
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ̇ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  Y 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             
                            be a boolean lattice algebra of events. Then the following two statements hold: 
                            
                               
                                  1. 
                                  
                                     
                                        X 
                                         is lattice isomorphic to a distributive algebra of events. 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  2. 
                                  
                                     
                                        Y 
                                         is event isomorphic to a boolean algebra of events.

Let  
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             
                            be a distributive lattice algebra and  
                            
                               Y 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  Y 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        ⋓ 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        ⋒ 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           – 
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ̇ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  Y 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             
                            be a boolean lattice algebra of events. Then the following two statements hold: 
                            
                               
                                  1. 
                                  
                                     
                                        X 
                                         is lattice isomorphic to a distributive algebra of events. 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  2. 
                                  
                                     
                                        Y 
                                         is event isomorphic to a boolean algebra of events.

X 
                                         is lattice isomorphic to a distributive algebra of events.

Y 
                                         is event isomorphic to a boolean algebra of events.

Proof 
                         
                            Stone (1936, 1937) .  □

Stone (1936, 1937) .  □

Note that in Statement 1 of  Theorem 1 , the isomorphism maps  
                         ⋓ 
                       into set-theoretic union,  
                         ∪ 
                      , and  
                         ⋒ 
                       into set-theoretic intersection,  
                         ∩ 
                      , and in Statement 2, the isomorphism maps  
                         
                            
                               ⋓ 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       into  
                         ∪ 
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               ⋒ 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       into  
                         ∩ 
                      , and  
                         
                            
                               – 
                               
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                       into set-theoretic intersection,  
                         − 
                      .

The following are some examples of boolean and distributive lattice algebras of events. In all cases,  
                         X 
                         ≠ 
                         ∅ 
                      .  
                         
                            1. 
                            For the power-set  
                                  ℘ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                of  
                                  X 
                               ,  
                                  
                                     〈 
                                     ℘ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        X 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     , 
                                     ∪ 
                                     , 
                                     ∩ 
                                     , 
                                     − 
                                     , 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     ∅ 
                                     〉 
                                  
                                is a boolean algebra of events. 
                         
                         
                            2. 
                            A  topology 
                               
                                  T 
                                 with  universe 
                               
                                  X 
                                is a set of subsets of  
                                  X 
                                such that  
                                  X 
                                  ∈ 
                                  T 
                               ,  
                                  ∅ 
                                  ∈ 
                                  T 
                               ,  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     A 
                                     ∩ 
                                     B 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  T 
                                for all  
                                  A 
                                and  
                                  B 
                                in  
                                  T 
                               , and  
                                  ⋃ 
                                  W 
                                is in  
                                  T 
                                 for each nonempty  
                                  W 
                                 such that  
                                  W 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  T 
                               . If  
                                  T 
                                 is a topology, then  
                                  
                                     〈 
                                     T 
                                     , 
                                     ⊆ 
                                     , 
                                     ∪ 
                                     , 
                                     ∩ 
                                     , 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     ∅ 
                                     〉 
                                  
                                is a distributive algebra of events.

                                  X

                               ,

                                  c

                                  X 
                                  =

                                        X

                                        X

                                           } 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           b

                                  ⋒ 
                                  ≠ 
                                  ∩ 
                               . Thus this is an example of a boolean lattice algebra of events that is not a boolean algebra of events. Let  
                                  Y

                                              z

                                           }

                                is a lattice isomorphism from  
                                  X 
                                 onto  
                                  Y 
                               . 
                         
                         
                            4. 
                            Let  
                                  X 
                                consists of the distinct elements  
                                  a 
                               ,  
                                  b 
                               , and

                                  ∅

                                  ∅

                                  X 
                                 has a complement.

                                  =

                                     ∅ 
                                     〉

                                  X 
                                 has no proper pair of disjoint events, that is,  
                                  A 
                                  ∩ 
                                  B 
                                  = 
                                  ∅ 
                                if and only if  
                                  A 
                                  = 
                                  ∅

                                  X 
                                and  
                                  ∅

                                  X 
                                 has a complement.

The complementation operation of a boolean algebra of events has the characteristics of being uniquely complemented and satisfying De Morgan’s Laws:

Definition 3 
                         Let  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        – 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ̇ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             be a complemented lattice algebra of events. Then the following two definitions hold.  
                               
                                  1. 
                                  
                                     
                                        X 
                                       is said to be  uniquely complemented  if and only if for all  
                                        A 
                                      and  
                                        B 
                                      in  
                                        X 
                                     , if  
                                        A 
                                        ⋒ 
                                        B 
                                        = 
                                        ∅ 
                                      and  
                                        A 
                                        ⋓ 
                                        B 
                                        = 
                                        X 
                                     , then  
                                        B 
                                        = 
                                        
                                           
                                              – 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                              ̇ 
                                           
                                        
                                        A

Proof 
                         Theorem 2.13 of  Narens (2015) .

Theorem 2.13 of  Narens (2015) .

Peirce (1880)  axiomatized the propositional calculus of logic by using a lattice algebra of events and a property formulated in terms of the complementation operation. He left out a proof of his axiomatization and later could not reproduce it when asked to do so by the logician Schröder. In the early 1900s, the mathematician Huntington planned an article for the  Transactions of the American Mathematical Society  presenting the various axiomatizations that existed at the time of the propositional calculus of logic. Peirce heard about this and sent in his earlier theorem, this time with a proof he subsequently found in papers related to 1880 article. In his article, Huntington presented Peirce’s theorem. Peirce’s axiomatization was flawed conceptually, because the property that he replaced distributivity with was unintuitive and it did not reflect symmetries inherent in lattice algebra of events, i.e., did not respect “duality”. In short, distributivity was a much better axiom than Peirce’s. Huntington, however, noticed merit in the overall argument and took an immediate consequence of unique complementation of Peirce’s property and added various complementation conditions to it to produce an axiomatization of the propositional calculus. These results led  Huntington (1904)  to conjecture that a boolean algebra of events could be axiomatized by only assuming a lattice algebra of events and unique complementation. After four decades of new instances of various uniquely complemented lattice algebras of events being shown to be boolean, Huntington’s conjecture that uniquely complemented lattice algebras of events were boolean was generally accepted to be true. However,  Dilworth (1945)  produced an incredibly complicated example of a uniquely complemented lattice algebra of events that was not boolean.

Theorems 3 – 7  below provide variations of Huntington’s conjecture that have been proven. The first is due to  Birkhoff and Ward (1939)  and requires the concept of “atomic lattice”.

Definition 4

                               X 
                               =

Theorem 3 
                         Birkhoff–Ward Theorem 
                         
                            Each uniquely complemented atomic lattice is boolean.

Each uniquely complemented atomic lattice is boolean.

Proof 
                         
                            Ogasawara and Saski (1949) .  □

Ogasawara and Saski (1949) .  □

Let  
                         X 
                         = 
                         
                            〈 
                            X 
                            , 
                            ⋓ 
                            , 
                            ⋒ 
                            , 
                            X 
                            , 
                            ∅ 
                            〉 
                         
                       be a lattice algebra of events. Then  
                         X 
                        is said to be  finite  if and only if  
                         X 
                        is a finite set. Note that by this definition  
                         X 
                        can be finite while  
                         X 
                       is infinite. It is a well-known theorem of lattice theory that each finite lattice algebra of events is atomic. The following is a consequence of this and the Birkhoff–Ward Theorem.

Theorem 4 
                         
                            Each uniquely complemented finite lattice algebra of events is boolean.  □

Each uniquely complemented finite lattice algebra of events is boolean.  □

Theorem 5  suggests considering non-uniquely complemented lattice algebras of events that satisfy De Morgan’s laws as candidates for probabilistic event spaces. This was the approach that Birkhoff & von Neumann took in their probabilistic logic for quantum mechanics. Their candidate had, in addition to De Morgan’s Laws, the following generalization of distributivity: For all events  
                         A 
                      ,  
                         B 
                      , and  
                         C 
                       in the algebra.  
                         
                            (1) 
                            
                               
                                  Modular Law : 
                               
                               if   
                               
                               A 
                               ⊆ 
                               B 
                                 then   
                               B 
                               ⋒ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  C 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  B 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  A 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ⋓ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  B 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  C 
                                  ) 
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                       The following theorem provides an alternate formulation of De Morgan’s laws for complemented lattice algebras of events.

In the lattice literature, a complementation operation satisfying De Morgan’s Laws are called an “orthocomplementation operation” and the special symbol  
                         
                            
                            
                               ⊥ 
                            
                         
                       is used to denote it. A lattice algebra of events with an orthocomplementation operation  
                         
                            
                            
                               ⊥ 
                            
                         
                       is called an “ortholattice”. If it is not a boolean algebra of events, then by  Theorem 5  it will have additional complementation operations. These additional operations need not be orthocomplementation operations. Formally, we have the following definition.

Definition 5 
                         
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈

                                     ⊥ 
                                  
                               
                             if and only if it is a lattice algebra of events and  
                               
                                  
                                  
                                     ⊥ 
                                  
                               
                             satisfies De Morgan’s Laws, that is, for all  
                               A 
                             and  
                               B

                                           ⊥ 
                                        
                                     
                                     .

X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  ,

                                  ,

Orthocomplementation operations play a major role in this article. Also playing a major role is a different notion of complementation, called “pseudo complementation”. It was employed by  Huntington (1904)  as part of his investigation of unique complementation. Huntington did not give pseudo complementation a formal designation because it was not an important concept at the time. Subsequently, it became important in the philosophy of mathematics where it provided a new form of logical negation that today is called “intuitionistic negation”.

Definition 6 
                         Let  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             be a lattice algebra of events. Then  
                               
                                  
                                     – 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                             is said to be a  pseudo complementation  operation on  
                               X 
                              if and only if it is a unary operation on  
                               X 
                              such that for all  
                               A 
                             and  
                               B 
                             in  
                               X 
                            , 
                               
                                  
                                     B 
                                     
                                     ⊆ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           – 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ̇ 
                                        
                                     
                                     A 
                                     
                                       iff   
                                     
                                     A 
                                     ⋒ 
                                     B 
                                     = 
                                     ∅ 
                                     . 
                                  
                               
                             It is easy to show that a lattice algebra of events  
                               X 
                              can have at most one pseudo complementation operation on it.

It is easy to show that the complementation operation on a boolean lattice algebra of events is a pseudo complementation operation. Huntington proved the following theorem.

Theorem 7 
                         
                            Suppose  
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           – 
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ̇ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             
                            is a complemented lattice where  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        – 
                                     
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                             
                            is also an operation of pseudo complementation on  
                            
                               X 
                            
                            . Then  
                            
                               X 
                                is boolean.

Suppose  
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           – 
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ̇ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             
                            is a complemented lattice where  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        – 
                                     
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                             
                            is also an operation of pseudo complementation on  
                            
                               X 
                            
                            . Then  
                            
                               X 
                                is boolean.

Proof 
                         Theorem 2.21 of  Narens (2015) .  □

Theorem 2.21 of  Narens (2015) .  □

Theorem 7  suggests two avenues for generalizing a boolean algebra of events: (1) Generalize to a complemented algebra of events that is not pseudo complemented, or (2) generalize to a pseudo complemented lattice algebra of events that is not complemented. This article pursues both avenues. The emphasis will be on finding generalizations that keep close to a boolean algebra of events to enhance chances for scientific applicability.

Birkhoff and von Neumann (1936)  took approach (1). They assumed an ortholattice and generalized distributivity to modularity (Eq.  (1) ). They applied their approach to the foundations of quantum mechanics. This article takes a more general approach to (1) than Birkhoff & von Neumann by keeping the ortholattice part and providing a different generalization of distributivity that is also a generalization of modularity.

In  Narens (2007, 2015) , I used the approach (2) to provide a new foundational theory for decision making. I retained the operations of  
                         ∪ 
                       and  
                         ∩ 
                       from a boolean algebra of events but replaced set-theoretic complementation,  
                         − 
                      , with a pseudo complementation operation. I developed a probability theory based on this approach and applied it to decision theory.

Starting in the 1920s, the mathematician Brouwer developed a radically different foundation for mathematics. One of its key tenets involved the nature of mathematical existence. In traditional mathematical logic, the statement  
                         
                            (2) 
                            
                               ∃ 
                               x 
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         
                       is read as,  
                         
                            (3) 
                            
                               “there exists an entity   
                               x 
                                 that has property   
                               F 
                               ” . 
                            
                         
                       Brouwer believed that mathematical existence should be constructive, so he interpreted Eq.  (2)  as,  
                         
                            (4) 
                            
                               “an entity   
                               x 
                                 can be found that has property   
                               F 
                               ” . 
                            
                         
                       In traditional mathematical logic,  
                         ¬ 
                       stands for “it is not the case that”, and the statement  
                         
                            (5) 
                            
                               ¬ 
                               
                               ∃ 
                               x 
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         
                       stands for,  
                         
                            (6) 
                            
                               “there does not exist an   
                               x 
                                 with property   
                               F 
                               ” , 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            (7) 
                            
                               ¬ 
                               
                               ¬ 
                               
                               ∃ 
                               x 
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               , 
                            
                         
                       stands for,  
                         
                            
                               “it is not the case that there does not exist an   
                               x 
                                 with property   
                               F 
                               ” , 
                            
                         
                       which in traditional mathematical logic is logically equivalent to Eq.  (3) . This is not the case for Brouwer’s interpretation of “existence”: Eq.  (2)  says an entity can be found that has property  
                         F 
                      , whereas Eq.  (7)  says that the assumption that  
                         ¬ 
                         
                         ∃ 
                         x 
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       leads to a contradiction.  
                         ¬ 
                         
                         ∃ 
                         x 
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       is in fact contradicted by Eq.  (2) . Brouwer interprets these contradictions as follows:  
                         
                            (8) 
                            
                               “The assumption of   
                               ∃ 
                               x 
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                                 implies   
                               ¬ 
                               
                               ¬ 
                               
                               ∃ 
                               x 
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ” . 
                            
                         
                       For Brouwer, this is obviously different from  
                         
                            (9) 
                            
                               “The assumption of   
                               ¬ 
                               
                               ¬ 
                               
                               ∃ 
                               x 
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                                 implies   
                               ∃ 
                               x 
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ” , 
                            
                         
                       because knowing  
                         ¬ 
                         
                         ¬ 
                         
                         ∃ 
                         x 
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      —i.e., knowing that the assumption that  
                         ¬ 
                         
                         ∃ 
                         x 
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       leads to a contradiction—does not provide a means for finding an  
                         x 
                       that satisfies  
                         F 
                      . Because of this, in Brouwer’s logic  
                         ¬ 
                         ∃ 
                         x 
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       is read as “the assumption  
                         ∃ 
                         x 
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       leads to a contradiction”.

The kind of logic that Brouwer used in making his derivations is called  intuitionistic logic , and its propositional logic was axiomatized by  Heyting (1930) . Two important properties of traditional logic are missing from it: The  Law of Contradiction ,  
                         
                            
                               ¬ 
                               
                               ¬ 
                               
                               α 
                                 implies   
                               α 
                               , 
                            
                         
                       and  Law of the Excluded Middle ,  
                         
                            
                               either   
                               α 
                                 is true or   
                               ¬ 
                               
                               α 
                                 is true . 
                            
                         
                       (Given the rest of Heyting’s axioms, the Law of Contradiction and the Law of the Excluded Middle are equivalent.)

The event space corresponding to intuitionistic propositional logic has the form of a pseudo complemented, distributive lattice,  
                         
                            〈 
                            X 
                            , 
                            ∪ 
                            , 
                            ∩ 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  – 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ̇ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            X 
                            , 
                            ∅ 
                            〉 
                         
                      . This event space will be used to form a generalization of boolean probability theory.

                       fail. In summary, the following properties hold for a pseudo complemented distributive algebra of events.

                      Topological examples   Let  
                         X 
                         =

                            X

                       be a distributive algebra of events. Then  
                         X 
                        is a topology if and only if for each nonempty set

                        of elements of  
                         X

                         ⋃ 
                         C 
                       is in  
                         X 
                      .

Suppose  
                         X 
                        is a topology. Define  
                         
                            
                               – 
                               
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                       on  
                         X 
                        as follows: For each  
                         A 
                       in  
                         X 
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     – 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                               A 
                               = 
                               ⋃ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  B 
                                  ∣ 
                                  A 
                                  ∩ 
                                  B 
                                  = 
                                  ∅ 
                                  } 
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                       Then using that  
                         X 
                        is distributive, it follows by an elementary argument that  
                         
                            
                               – 
                               
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                       is an operation of pseudo complementation on  
                         X 
                      . An interesting consequence of this is that since each finite distributive algebra  
                         D 
                        of events is a topology, it is pseudo complemented.

Topologies are well-investigated mathematical structures. Because of this, they provide concrete examples and insights about pseudo complemented distributive algebras of events. They can also provide counterexamples to various equalities that hold in boolean algebras of events. For example, Statements 4, 6, and 9 are generalizations of the identities  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     – 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                               A 
                               ∪ 
                               A 
                               = 
                               X 
                               , 
                               
                               A 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     – 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     – 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                               A 
                               , 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     – 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                               A 
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  
                                     – 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                               B 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     – 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ∩ 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               , 
                            
                         
                       for boolean algebras of events with sure event  
                         X

An obvious criterion for refuting an event  
                            A 
                          is for the assumption that the true state of the world is in  
                            A 
                          produces a contradiction. There are many ways of contradicting “true state of the world is in

                            A

                         . An important use of this concept of “refutation” is that it gives an explicit, mathematical interpretation the logical interplay between the philosophical concepts of verification and refutation. In particular, it suggests classifying events in  
                            X 
                           into three categories: those that can be verified, those that can be refuted, and those whose refutations can be refuted. That is, each event
