Proof 
                            Theorem 3.16 of  Narens (2015) .  □

Theorem 3.16 of  Narens (2015) .  □

Boolean algebras of events are special cases of pseudo complemented distributive algebra of events—cases where the pseudo complement operator is a complementation operator. This makes pseudo complemented algebras of events a richer class of algebraic structures than boolean algebras of events.  Theorem 9  displays one aspect of this richness. This richness will be exploited in applications presented later.

There are many reasons that boolean probability theory is effective in scientific applications. An important one is that the underlying boolean algebra of events is sufficiently rich in disjoint events to make finite additivity a useful concept. In general, a distributive algebra of events need not have a rich family of disjoint events. For example, the pseudo complemented distributive algebra of events consisting of the  
                         n 
                         + 
                         2 
                       events,  
                         ∅ 
                         , 
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         , 
                         … 
                         , 
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         , 
                         X 
                      , where  
                         ∅ 
                         ⊂ 
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         ⊂ 
                         ⋯ 
                         ⊂ 
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         ⊂ 
                         X 
                      , has no pair of nontrivial disjoint events. A boolean algebra of events has a rich family of disjoint events, because for events  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       in the algebra, the event  
                         A 
                         − 
                         B 
                       is also in the algebra. This allows  
                         A 
                         ∪ 
                         B 
                       to be decomposed into the disjoint union of three events,  
                         
                            ( 
                            A 
                            − 
                            B 
                            ) 
                         
                         ∪ 
                         
                            ( 
                            B 
                            − 
                            A 
                            ) 
                         
                         ∪ 
                         
                            ( 
                            A 
                            ∩ 
                            B 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Because such a decomposition need not hold in a distributive algebra of events, the definition of “boolean probability function” has to be generalized for it to be a useful probabilistic concept for general distributive algebras of events. This is accomplished in the following definition.

Definition 8 
                         Let  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∪ 
                                  , 
                                  ∩ 
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             be a distributive algebra of events. Then the function  
                               P 
                              on  
                               X 
                              is said to be a  distributive probability function  on  
                               X 
                             if and only if the following two conditions hold for all  
                               A 
                             and  
                               B 
                             in  
                               X 
                            :  
                               
                                  1. 
                                  
                                     
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           X 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                     ,  
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           ∅ 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        0 
                                     , and  
                                        0 
                                        ≤ 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ≤ 
                                        1 
                                     . 
                               
                               
                                  2. 
                                  
                                     Distributive finite additivity :  
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ∪ 
                                           B 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        + 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           B 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        − 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ∩ 
                                           B 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     . □

Let  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∪ 
                                  , 
                                  ∩ 
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             be a distributive algebra of events. Then the function  
                               P 
                              on  
                               X 
                              is said to be a  distributive probability function  on  
                               X 
                             if and only if the following two conditions hold for all  
                               A 
                             and  
                               B 
                             in  
                               X 
                            :  
                               
                                  1. 
                                  
                                     
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           X 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                     ,  
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           ∅ 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        0 
                                     , and  
                                        0 
                                        ≤ 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ≤ 
                                        1 
                                     . 
                               
                               
                                  2. 
                                  
                                     Distributive finite additivity :  
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ∪ 
                                           B 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        + 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           B 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        − 
                                        P

                                           A

                                           B 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     . □

                                        0

                                        0

                                        1 
                                     .

The following theorem shows that the concept of a “distributive probability function” coincides with the concept of a “boolean probability function” on a boolean algebra of events.

Theorem 10

                               =

                                  ,

                                  ,

Although a distributive probability function may not be a boolean probability function on a distributive algebra of events, there may be subalgebras for which it may be boolean. An important example of this is a stone lattice.

Let

                         =

As discussed in Section   7.2  below, the following theorem is important for establish the rationality of distributive probability theory.

Theorem 4.16 of  Narens (2015) .

The generation-recognition theory of recall is one of the simplest memory models. It assumes that when a subject is asked to perform a recall, for example, asked to name the kinds of wild African animals, she generates a set of animal names (the generation phase) and selects from those names the ones she believes names wild African animals (the recognition phase). In contrast, an example of a recognition task is where a subject is presented a list of wild animal names that includes names of wild African animals and the subject is asked to select those that name wild African animals. In a recognition task, the need for the subject to generate possible items is eliminated. For most situations, this makes, in terms of percent correct responses, the recognition task easier than the corresponding recall task.

Narens (2009)  models the relationship between recognition and recall in terms of pseudo complementation. For example, suppose  X  is a set of animal names and  
                            C 
                          is a set of category descriptions of names from  X , for example, suppose the description “African animal”, denoted by the symbol  A , is in  
                            C 
                         . For each category description  C  in  
                            C 
                         , suppose  
                            C 
                          is the subset of names of animals the subject recalls when presented  C . By definition, let  
                            
                               
                                  – 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ̇ 
                               
                            
                            C 
                          be the set of names that is recognized by the subject as being instances of the description  not C , for example, for  
                            
                               C 
                            
                            = 
                            
                               A 
                            
                         , the set of names of animals that are recognized as being non-African. Then  
                            
                               
                                  – 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ̇ 
                               
                            
                            
                               
                                  – 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ̇ 
                               
                            
                            A 
                          is the set of animal names that is recognized as being described by  not not A , which is assumed to be the same as the set of names as being recognized as instances of  
                            
                               A 
                            
                         . Suppose that the recognition of African animal names is strictly easier than the recall of African animal names. Then in this example,

Various arguments have been put forth in the economic and philosophic literatures claiming that boolean probability theory is the only rational theory of probability. These arguments consist in  assuming  an underlying boolean algebra of events and  arguing for  the algebraic properties of a boolean probability function. The serious part of the arguments is showing that boolean finite additivity is necessary for rationality. Using the following reasoning,  Theorem 11  shows that such arguments also apply to situations where only a distributive algebra of events characterize the underlying event space:

Suppose person  
                            Q 
                          assigns probabilities to a distributive algebra of events  
                            X 
                            = 
                            
                               〈 
                               X 
                               , 
                               ∪ 
                               , 
                               ∩ 
                               , 
                               X 
                               , 
                               ∅ 
                               〉 
                            
                          using the distributive probability function  
                            P 
                         . Then, by  Theorem 11 , in theory there could exist a rational person  
                            R 
                          who assigns, in a rational way, probabilities to the boolean algebra of events  
                            
                               〈 
                               ℘ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  ) 
                               
                               , 
                               ∪ 
                               , 
                               ∩ 
                               , 
                               − 
                               , 
                               X 
                               , 
                               ∅ 
                               〉 
                            
                          using the boolean probability function  
                            
                               
                                  P 
                               
                               
                                  ⋆ 
                               
                            
                          that is an extension of  
                            P 
                         . Then rationality requires  
                            R 
                         ’s assignments of probabilities to  
                            X 
                           to be rational, and therefore  
                            Q 
                         ’s assignments of probabilities to  
                            X 
                           must too be rational. In particular, the economic and philosophic conditions in the literature for establishing the irrationality of assignments would apply to  
                            Q 
                         ’s assignments, that is,  
                            Q 
                         ’s assignments are not irrational (because  
                            R 
                         ’s assignments are not irrational). Economic and philosophic conditions for rationality are based on the idea that no irrational behavior is detected. Deeper presentations of this result about rationality can be found in Chapter 4 of  Narens (2015)  for the Dutch Book Argument, and in  Narens (2016a)  for arguments based on subjective expected utility.

This subsection presents two applications of pseudo complementation. The first is for a foundation for Tversky’s and colleagues’ theory of probability judgments, known in the literature as “support theory”. The second is for a collection of theories of decision called “multimode decision theory”. The idea behind both kinds of theories is that in typical experiments involving probabilistic judgments, subjects base their choices on their cognitive interpretations of the probabilistic situations. Because these interpretations are sensitive to the experimental context that the subject is put in, this can result in the subject producing cross-context behaviors that violate concepts of rationality. This presents the following modeling challenge: Develop theories about relationships among contexts to explain the violations of rationality. Such a development should be helpful for understanding human decision behavior and for designing new and informative decision paradigms.

Numerous empirical studies conducted by Amos Tversky and colleagues show that the sum of the judged probabilities of a description  A  of an event  
                               A 
                             is often significantly smaller than the probabilities of sum of the descriptions of a partition of  
                               A 
                             into three or more events. The distinction between descriptions of events and the events they described is particularly important in Support Theory and other paradigms where conclusions are based on empirical results where different descriptions of the same event lead subjects to make different choices. The following is a typical kind of Support Theory experiment where this happens.

(Jones Versus Clinton) 
                            Fox and Birke (2002)  recruited 200 practicing attorneys (median reported experience: 17 years) at a national meeting of the American Bar Association (in November 1997). Of this group, 98% reported that they knew at least “a little” about the sexual harassment allegation made by Paula Jones against President Clinton. At the time of that survey, the case could have been disposed of by either  
                               A 
                            , which was an outcome other than a judicial verdict, or  
                               B 
                            , which was a judicial verdict. Furthermore, outcomes other than a judicial verdict can partition  
                               A 
                             into  
                               
                                  (A1) 
                                  settlement; 
                               
                               
                                  (A2) 
                                  dismissal as a result of judicial action; 
                               
                               
                                  (A3) 
                                  legislative grant of immunity to Clinton; and 
                               
                               
                                  (A4) 
                                  withdrawal of the claim by Jones.

settlement;

dismissal as a result of judicial action;

legislative grant of immunity to Clinton; and

withdrawal of the claim by Jones.

Each attorney was randomly assigned to judge the probability of one of these six events. The results are given in  Table 1 
                            .

The Jones versus Clinton example illustrates a core idea of the much investigated empirically based theory of probability judgments called  Support Theory . This was initially formulated in  Tversky and Koehler (1994)  and later modified by  Rottenstreich and Tversky (1997) . Chapter 10 of  Narens (2007)  provided a foundation for it formulated in terms of a pseudo complemented distributive algebra of events.

A central tenet of Support Theory is that in making a judgment of probability, a subject employs the cognitive heuristics of availability and representativeness that Kahneman and Tversky described in many seminal articles (e.g.,  Tversky & Kahneman, 1974 ). This article considers only the  availability heuristic , although its methods of modeling also applies to the representativeness heuristic. (See Chapter 10 of   Narens, 2007 , for examples and a discussion.)  Tversky and Kahneman (1974)  describe the availability heuristic as follows:  
                               There are situations in which people assess the frequency of a class or the probability of an event by the ease with which instances or occurrences can be brought to mind. For example, one may assess the risk of heart attack among middle-aged people by recalling occurrences among one’s acquaintances. Similarly, one may evaluate the probability that a given business venture will fail by imagining various difficulties it could encounter. This judgmental heuristic is called availability. Availability is a useful clue for assessing frequency or probability, because instances of large classes are usually recalled better and faster than instances of less frequent classes. However, availability is affected by factors other than frequency and probability.  p.  1127.

At the foundational level, a major difference between Narens’ approach and that of Tversky and colleagues is that Narens models interpretations of descriptions as events in a pseudo complemented distributive algebra of events, whereas Tversky and colleagues model them in a boolean algebra of events. In Narens’ theory the properties of pseudo complementation,  
                               
                                  
                                     A 
                                     ⊂ 
                                     
                                        
                                           – 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ̇

                                           ̇ 
                                        
                                     
                                     A 
                                     
                                     and 
                                     
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        A 
                                        ∩

                                        = 
                                        ∅

                                        ∩

                                        = 
                                        ∅ 
                                        )

                             play a major role. The following is an abbreviated version of Support Theory based on Narens’ approach.

When a subject makes a probability judgment about a description  E  of an event, Support Theory assumes that this is done by the subject assessing the  support for  the true state of the world being in the event described by  
                               
                                  E 
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     + 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     E 
                                  
                                  )

                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     E 
                                  
                                  )

                                     E

                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     E 
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             are assumed to be nonnegative real numbers corresponding to subjective strength of support.  Note that the support for and the support against are defined for descriptions of events and not for events.  This allows two descriptions of the same event to produce different assessments of support for or against it. Because of this, different descriptions of the same event can lead to different probability judgments.

A basic Support Theory experimental paradigm is to ask the subject to judge “the probability of

                             occurring rather than  
                               
                                  B 
                               
                             occurring”, in symbols  
                               P

                                  )

                             implicitly assumes that the true state of the world is in the event described by

                            .)

Eq.  (12)  is a modest generalization of a formula that  Luce (1959)  and others used to model choice situations where  
                               P 
                             is an observed probability function instead of a subjective estimation.  Rottenstreich and Tversky (1997)  generalized Luce’s formulation so that it would apply to situations where a subject’s probability estimations violated boolean finite additivity.

Assessments of support using the availability heuristic are based on the number and ease that instances of  CI ( E ) (or  CC ( E )) are brought to mind. The most basic tenet of Support Theory is that the partitioning of the description  A  in the judgment  
                               P 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     A 
                                  
                                  : 
                                  
                                     B 
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             generally increases the judged probability. This phenomenon was illustrated in the Jones vs. Clinton example and often appears in empirical studies of probability judgments. Support Theory usually describes it as follows: If  C or D  describes a partition of  A , that is,  
                               
                                  (i) 
                                  the states of the world  linguistically  described by  A  equals the states of the world  linguistically  described by  C or D , 
                               
                               
                                  (ii) 
                                  the set-theoretic intersection of the states of the world  linguistically  described by  C  with the states of the world  linguistically  described by  D  is empty, and 
                               
                               
                                  (iii) 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           CI 
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              C 
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ∩ 
                                        
                                           CI 
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              B 
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        ∅ 
                                      and  
                                        
                                           CI 
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              D 
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ∩ 
                                        
                                           CI 
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              B 
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        ∅ 
                                     , 
                               
                             then usually,  
                               
                                  (17) 
                                  
                                     P

                                           B 
                                        
                                        )

                             In terms of the cognitive theory presented here, Eq.  (17)  follows from (iii) and

Narens (2007)  models additional heuristics and decision processes involving ambiguity and vagueness through representations in topological algebras of events.

The above demonstrates that various psychological concepts derivable from algebraic properties of pseudo complemented distributive algebras of events have structural correspondences with notions from cognitive psychology. Cognitive psychologists and some economists avoid using boolean probability theory for measuring uncertainty by assuming a boolean algebra of events and using a system of weights on events instead of probabilities. However, such a weighting system does not have sufficient logical structure to provide a rich mathematical calculus for manipulating and calculating measurements of uncertainty. It is precisely having such a calculus that makes boolean probability theory so useful in applications. Pseudo complemented distributive algebras of events with distributive probability functions also have a rich probabilistic calculus.

The DSEU model is a special cases of what  Narens (2016a)  calls  multimode utility theory (MUT) . MUT is a generalization of the  Conditional SEU utility theory (CSEU)  of decision making of  Luce and Krantz (1971) , which in turn is a generalization of the SEU model of  Savage (1954) . MUT incorporates emotion, bias, and other modes that can influence utility judgments for lotteries. In MUT, judgments are based on subjective interpretations that often vary with the mode, resulting in the same event having multiple interpretations and the decision maker making mode-dependent judgments. MUT contains many kinds of models, and the one presented in this subsection–DSEU–is very close to Conditional SEU. I consider it to be a boundedly rational version of Conditional SEU. Besides having the decision maker enter into different modes while making decisions, MUT models employ a pseudo complemented distributive algebra of events instead of a boolean algebra of events for modeling its event structure.

CSEU assumes that the decision maker has a utility function  
                            u 
                          over outcomes and lotteries and a boolean probability function  
                            P 
                           over events such that for a lottery

SEU and CSEU differ in the sets of lotteries to which they apply: In SEU, all the lotteries are based on the same sure event—the union of all the events occurring in a lottery. In CSEU, different lotteries can be based on different sure events. Eq.  (19)  is used to evaluate both. In SEU,  
                            
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  = 
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                            
                               
                                  A 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          is held fixed, in CSEU it is allowed to vary with lottery and  
                            n 
                         .

Although SEU has been used as the standard for rational decision making throughout science, its adequacy for decisions where risk, ambiguity, or vagueness play significant roles have been questioned, for example, in the famous counterexamples provided by  Allais (1953)  and  Ellsberg (1961) . These counterexamples, and much behavioral experimental research based on them, led to many behavioral-economic models of human decision making, prospect theory of  Kahneman and Tversky (1979)  currently being the most prominent. The DSEU model was designed by  Narens (2016a)  in order to accomplish the following:

It takes SEU as the gold standard for rationality, and expands it by introducing new primitive concepts for emotion and other kinds of subjective modes that can influence decision making. The article’s goal is threefold:  
                            
                               • 
                               Expand SEU in a manner that incorporates these subjective modes, 
                            
                            
                               • 
                               investigate the normativeness and rationality of the expansion, and 
                            
                            
                               • 
                               show that the expansion is applicable to important issues in decision theory. ( Narens, 2016a .)

Expand SEU in a manner that incorporates these subjective modes,

investigate the normativeness and rationality of the expansion, and

show that the expansion is applicable to important issues in decision theory. ( Narens, 2016a .)

Narens uses the framework described in  Fig. 1 
                          for the psychological modeling of decisions involving lotteries. The decision making starts with a set of lotteries  
                            L 
                         . Each lottery  
                            L 
                          in  
                            L 
                           has a description  L , and the set of these descriptions is denoted by  
                            L 
                         . Two kinds of interpretations of descriptions in  
                            L 
                          enter into the modeling: (1) an  objective interpretation  that interprets  L  as  
                            L 
                         , and (2) an  subjective interpretation  denoted by  
                            I 
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  L 
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          which is the mental representation of  L  that the subject uses in making his decision involving  L , i.e., an interpretation of  L  as a  subjective lottery . In the process of making a decision concerning  L , the subject’s mode may change, which in turn may produce a change in the subjective probability that he assigns to a description of an event. The subject uses his subjective interpretations of the descriptions of lotteries to make a decision, e.g., which lottery has best value to him. This decision leads to an intended action that leads to an observable action in the objective world, e.g., the subject points to the lottery in  
                            L 
                           he wants to receive.

Fig. 2 
                          shows the difference between CSEU (left panel) and DSEU (right panel) in terms of “rationality”. In the left panel, rationality (the double arrow) is expressed as relationship between objective actions and objective lotteries. CSEU is formulated in terms of such a relationship. In the right panel, subjective rationality (the double arrow) is expressed as a relationship between intended actions and subjective lotteries. The DSEU model is formulated to capture this kind rationality. It is formulated in a manner that employs the same formula for calculating utility (Eq.  (19) ) that CSEU uses. The following are differences between them:  
                            
                               1. 
                               In CSEU event descriptions are represented in a boolean algebra of events, while in DSEU they are represented in a pseudo complemented distributive algebra of events. 
                            
                            
                               2. 
                               Where the double arrow is to be placed in  Fig. 2 . 
                            
                            
                               3. 
                               In CSEU, probabilities are not influenced by outcomes, while in DSEU they can be. That is, if  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              a 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   and  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              a 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   are descriptions of the terms  
                                     
                                        
                                           a 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           A 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   and  
                                     
                                        
                                           a 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           A 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   that are from different lotteries  
                                     
                                        
                                           L 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   and  
                                     
                                        
                                           L 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   of  
                                     L 
                                    having descriptions,

                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   and  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              L 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   respectively and  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                  , then in DSEU the probability assigned to  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   in the computation of the utility of  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              L 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   need not be the same as the probability assigned to  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   in the computation of the utility of  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              L 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                  . The reason for this is that features of  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              a 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                  , e.g., it describes a catastrophe, may put the subject in a mode  
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   during his cognitive processing of the term  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              a 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A

                                   in his computing of the utility of  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              L 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   that is different from the mode  
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   that his cognitive processing of  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              a 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   puts him into during his computing of the utility of  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              L 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                  .

In CSEU event descriptions are represented in a boolean algebra of events, while in DSEU they are represented in a pseudo complemented distributive algebra of events.

Where the double arrow is to be placed in  Fig. 2 .

In CSEU, probabilities are not influenced by outcomes, while in DSEU they can be. That is, if  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              a 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A

                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   are descriptions of the terms  
                                     
                                        
                                           a 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           A 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   and  
                                     
                                        
                                           a 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           A 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   that are from different lotteries  
                                     
                                        
                                           L 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   and  
                                     
                                        
                                           L 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   of  
                                     L 
                                    having descriptions,  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              L 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   and  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              L

                                   respectively and  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                  , then in DSEU the probability assigned to  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                   in the computation of the utility of

Formally, the model DSEU of  
                            L 
                          consists of the following.  
                            
                               • 
                               The set  
                                     A 
                                   of descriptions of events that occur in some description  L  of  
                                     L 
                                  . 
                            
                            
                               • 
                               The set  
                                     OUT 
                                   of descriptions of outcomes that occur in some description  L  of  
                                     L 
                                  . 
                            
                            
                               • 
                               A description of each lottery  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              L 
                                           
                                           = 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       a

                                                    1 
                                                 
                                              
                                              ,

                                     L

                                     ∪ 
                                     L

(4) is a coherence condition. It provides a rule that relates all subjective interpretations of an event across lotteries in  
                            L 
                         . In particular, by Statement 10 of  Theorem 8  and the other above conditions, it implies that the interpretations of disjoint events are disjoint and thus that the subjective interpretations of lotteries have the formal properties of lotteries.

Note that the utility of outcomes in Eq.  (20)  does not depend on mode. Thus the influence of mode is completely dependent on the  probability weighting functions 
                         
                            P 
                            
                               [ 
                               
                                  
                                     I 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           A 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                               ] 
                            
                         . Because  
                            P 
                           is a distributive probability function, it is reasonable to also call  
                            P 
                           a  subjective probability function .

Obvious applications of DSEU are to situations where an outcome can be pivotal in instilling hope, e.g., a lottery ticket with a chance of a huge payout ( Lopes, 1987 ), or instilling fear, e.g., a catastrophic outcome ( Chichilnisky, 2009 ). Several theories of utility (e.g.,   Kahneman & Tversky, 1979; Luce, 1991 ) distinguish gains from losses (e.g.   Luce, 1991, 2000 ). For example, for gains versus losses, each event has two interpretations, one for a gain and one for a loss. The following are two big differences between DSEU and most models in the literature:  
                            
                               (1) 
                               In DSEU, deviations from SEU are explained in terms of the structure of the mental event space instead of a psychologically external boolean algebra of events. 
                            
                            
                               (2) 
                               As discussed below, the decisions that a subject makes involving subjective experience and conforming to DSEU are consistent with rationality, but non-SEU decision models in the literature have utility functions or probability functions that violate properties of rationality.

In DSEU, deviations from SEU are explained in terms of the structure of the mental event space instead of a psychologically external boolean algebra of events.

As discussed below, the decisions that a subject makes involving subjective experience and conforming to DSEU are consistent with rationality, but non-SEU decision models in the literature have utility functions or probability functions that violate properties of rationality.

Narens (2016a)  makes the following comments about rationality and SEU and DSEU:

The rationality arguments for SEU were created to show that for the phenomena that SEU were designed to model, SEU was rational. They need not apply to rational decision making for other kinds of phenomena. Because of this, several of the assumptions in rationality arguments for SEU do not apply MUT and its model DSEU. In particular, the following has been noted:  
                            
                               1. 
                               The event algebras of SEU and DSEU are different. The rationality arguments for SEU assume a boolean algebra of events. To my knowledge, a well-reasoned attempt to justify this assumption has never been made in the literature. This assumption fails in some DSEU models: DSEU assumes a pseudo complemented distributive algebra of events. 
                            
                            
                               2. 
                               The lotteries under consideration are different. The lottery terms in SEU consist of all possible pairs of outcome and events. This assumption, which is used in rationality arguments for SEU, is not rationally justified. It fails in some DSEU models. 
                            
                            
                               3. 
                               The rationality arguments for SEU assume an objective world and that the decision maker agrees with the objective occurrence/nonoccurence of events. This assumption fails in some DSEU models. 
                            
                            
                               4. 
                               The rationality arguments for SEU assume a decision maker who has objective interpretations of descriptions of events. This assumption fails in some DSEU models. It occurs in DSEU as only as a degenerate case. 
                            
                            
                               5. 
                               The rationality arguments for SEU assume the buying and selling price of outcomes coincide. This is not assumed in all MUT situations.

The event algebras of SEU and DSEU are different. The rationality arguments for SEU assume a boolean algebra of events. To my knowledge, a well-reasoned attempt to justify this assumption has never been made in the literature. This assumption fails in some DSEU models: DSEU assumes a pseudo complemented distributive algebra of events.

The lotteries under consideration are different. The lottery terms in SEU consist of all possible pairs of outcome and events. This assumption, which is used in rationality arguments for SEU, is not rationally justified. It fails in some DSEU models.

The rationality arguments for SEU assume an objective world and that the decision maker agrees with the objective occurrence/nonoccurence of events. This assumption fails in some DSEU models.

The rationality arguments for SEU assume a decision maker who has objective interpretations of descriptions of events. This assumption fails in some DSEU models. It occurs in DSEU as only as a degenerate case.

The rationality arguments for SEU assume the buying and selling price of outcomes coincide. This is not assumed in all MUT situations.

Despite these differences, from the perspective of rationality arguments for SEU, DSEU automatically retains a good deal of rationality: By  Theorem 12  below, under standard background assumptions DSEU models are embeddable in SEU models. This implies that much of SEU rationality transfers to DSEU.

Proof 
                            Theorem 9.3 of  Narens (2007) .  □

Theorem 9.3 of  Narens (2007) .  □

Narens (2016a)  provides the following argument for the rationality of the behavior of a person satisfying DSEU:  
                            Humans are emotional. And while some psychologists may designate certain human emotional states as “neutral”, these “neutral” states do not appear to have much to do with rational or normative decision making. DSEU shows how shifting affect can impact subjective probabilities while yielding coherent decision making. I conclude from this result that it is possible to include affect into normative decision making–or put differently–to redefine normative decision making to include affect. In my opinion, such an inclusion allows for a better decision theory for humans, both from normative and descriptive perspectives, than those, like SEU, that ignore affect. 
                            Humans are also subjective. In traditional decision theories, subjectivity is treated like a crazed person in need of a straight jacket. In SEU, the straight jacket takes the form of a rigid structure that only allows subjectivity for the importance of outcomes and the size of probabilities. In contrast, MUT sees instead a colorful individual in need of the kind of clarity provided by, for example, DSEU.

Distributive, modular, and orthomodular are the three of the most important classes of lattices. They are found throughout mathematics and physics. They also have the right algebraic properties to be domains of probability functions. This section provides definitions and some of their basic properties.

The following theorem gives alternative definitions of “distributive” ( Definition 2 ) and “modular” (Eq.  (1) ). The alternative definition of “modular” provides a better insight into what is being assumed in ortholattices by its generalization “orthomodularity”.

                                     , then   
                                     A 
                                     = 
                                     B 
                                     ⋓ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        A 
                                        ⋒ 
                                        
                                           
                                              B 
                                           
                                           
                                              ⊥ 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     . 
                                  
                               
                            
                            □

Theorem 14 
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               X 
                                be an ortholattice. Then   (1)   if  
                            
                               X 
                                is distributive, then it is modular, and   (2)   if  
                            
                               X 
                                is modular then it is orthomodular.
