There are many examples and applications of modular lattices in mathematics. For probability theory, the one that stands out is the use of subspaces of a Hilbert space as event spaces for quantum phenomena in physics.  von Neumann (1932)  provided a systematic foundational study for this topic. It was formalized in the seminal article, “The Logic of Quantum Mechanics”, by  Birkhoff and von Neumann (1936) . In their analysis of the role of boolean algebras of events in probability theory, Birkhoff and von Neumann concluded that,  
                         The main difference seems to be that whereas logicians have usually assumed that properties [of orthocomplementation] of negation were the ones least able to withstand a critical analysis, the study of mechanics points to the  distributive identities  [i.e., distributivity and  
                               ∪ 
                            -distributivity] as the weakest link in the algebra of logic. ( p.   837.) 
                       They suggested that quantum mechanics required a modular ortholattice for its logic instead of a boolean lattice algebra of events. They had a particular ortholattice in mind: finite dimensional Hilbert space.  Husimi (1937)  noted that this seemed strange, since  von Neumann (1932)  in his  Mathematische Grundlagen der Quantenmechanik  needed an infinite dimensional Hilbert Space in his formulation. This led Husimi to propose replacing Birkhoff’s and von Neumann’s logics for quantum mechanics with orthomodular lattices. Husimi’s view ultimately won out, and today orthomodular lattices are often called “quantum logics”.

A Hilbert space is an inner product complete vector space  
                         V 
                       over the real or complex numbers. Vectors  
                         a 
                       and  
                         b 
                       in  
                         V 
                       are said to be  orthogonal , in symbols,  
                         a 
                         ⊥ 
                         b 
                       if and only if the inner product of  
                         a 
                       and  
                         b 
                       is 0. If  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       are subspaces of  
                         V 
                      , then  
                         A 
                       is said to be  orthogonal to 
                      
                         B 
                      , in symbols,  
                         A 
                         ⊥ 
                         B 
                      , if and only if for each  
                         a 
                       in  
                         A 
                       and each  
                         b 
                       in  
                         B 
                      ,  
                         a 
                         ⊥ 
                         b 
                      , and the  orthogonal complement  of  
                         A 
                      , in symbols,  
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               ⊥ 
                            
                         
                      , is the set of all vectors  
                         c 
                       in  
                         V 
                       such that  
                         c 
                         ⊥ 
                         a 
                       for each  
                         a 
                       in  
                         A 
                      . It is well-known that for each subspace  
                         A 
                       of  
                         V 
                      , that  
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               ⊥ 
                            
                         
                       is a subspace of  
                         V 
                      . For subspaces  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                      , the  span  of  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                      , in symbols,  
                         A 
                         ⋓ 
                         B 
                      , is the smallest subspace of  
                         V 
                       such that  
                         A 
                         ⊆ 
                         A 
                         ⋓ 
                         B 
                       and  
                         B 
                         ⊆ 
                         A 
                         ⋓ 
                         B 
                      . It is easy to verify that such a subspace  
                         A 
                         ⋓ 
                         B 
                       always exists.

For a finite dimensional Hilbert space  
                         V 
                      , each subspace  
                         A 
                       is closed under the topology induced by the inner product metric. Infinite dimensional Hilbert spaces have some subspaces that are not closed.

Let  
                         C 
                        be the set of all  closed subspaces  of a Hilbert space  
                         H 
                       with set of vectors  
                         V 
                       and let  
                         C 
                         = 
                         
                            〈 
                            C 
                            , 
                            ⋓ 
                            , 
                            ∩ 
                            
                               
                                  , 
                               
                               
                                  ⊥ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            V 
                            , 
                            ∅ 
                            〉 
                         
                      . It is well-known that if  
                         C 
                        is finite dimensional, then it is an ortholattice satisfying modularity, and if  
                         C 
                        is infinite dimensional, then it is an orthomodular lattice.

Suppose  
                         C 
                        is finite dimensional. For each  
                         A 
                       in  
                         C 
                      , let  
                         
                            
                               d 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                       the dimension of   
                                     A 
                                  
                                  
                                     the dimension of   
                                     V 
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                       It is well-known that  
                         d 
                         
                            ( 
                            A 
                            ) 
                         
                       satisfies the equation,  
                         
                            (21) 
                            
                               d 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               d 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ) 
                               
                               + 
                               d 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               − 
                               d 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ∩ 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                       Thus  
                         d 
                       would satisfy the formal properties of a distributive probability function if  
                         ⋓ 
                       were set-theoretic union,  
                         ∪ 
                      . This makes  
                         d 
                       appear to be a reasonable candidate for a generalization of distributive probability theory. It turns out that it is not a very good one.

For a distributive algebra of events, the version of Eq.  (21)  with  
                         ⋓ 
                         = 
                         ∪ 
                       captures an adequate definition of the“additivity” of probabilities for the  
                         ⋓ 
                      -least upper bound of two events. For modular, non-distributive lattices, Eq.  (21)  does not. The literature uses a different equation to capture the additivity of probabilities of distinguished pairs of disjoint events for modular ortholattices. This latter form of “additivity” is discussed later in Section   9 .

In lattice theory, Eq.  (21)  is used for lattice algebras to define a class of lattices known as “metric lattices”. For this class of lattices, the generalized version becomes a metric or, alternatively, a way of measuring the “height” of an element of a lattice. In this context, “height” corresponds roughly to the concept of the dimension of a subspace of a vector space.

The following is a well-known theorem of lattice theory.

                            Let  
                            
                               X 
                                be a metric lattice algebra. Then  
                            
                               X 
                                is modular.

Modular and distributive lattice algebra of events can be characterized by requiring that they have no sublattices of particular types. These characterizations are useful in proofs and provide insights into probability theories based on lattices. For example, the lack of sublattices of the type described in  Fig. 3 
                       provides a useful means for determining the modularity/non-modularity of a lattice.

The following characterization of modular lattices is due to  Dedekind (1900) .

Theorem 2.12 of  Narens (2015) .  □

There is a probability theory for ortholattices that works well for distributive, modular, and orthomodular lattices and is applicable to a wide range of empirical situations. It is aptly called “orthoprobability theory”, and the existence of orthocomplements guarantees it sufficient probabilistic additivity.

Definition 12 
                         Let  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  
                                     
                                        , 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⊥ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             be an ortholattice. The  
                               P 
                              is said to be an  orthoprobability function  on  
                               X 
                              if and only if the following two conditions hold for all  
                               A 
                             and  
                               B 
                             in  
                               X 
                            :  
                               
                                  1. 
                                  
                                     
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           X 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                     ,  
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           ∅ 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        0 
                                     , and  
                                        0 
                                        ≤ 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ≤ 
                                        1 
                                     . 
                               
                               
                                  2. 
                                  
                                     Ortho finite additivity : If  
                                        A 
                                        ⊆ 
                                        
                                           
                                              B 
                                           
                                           
                                              ⊥ 
                                           
                                        
                                     , then  
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ∪ 
                                           B 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        + 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           B 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     . 
                               
                             An orthoprobability function  
                               P 
                              is said to be  positive  if and only if for all  
                               A 
                             and  
                               B 
                             in  
                               X 
                            , if  
                               A 
                               ⊂ 
                               B 
                             then  
                               P 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ) 
                               
                               < 
                               P 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                            .  □

Let  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  ,

                                        , 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⊥ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             be an ortholattice. The  
                               P 
                              is said to be an  orthoprobability function  on  
                               X

                               A 
                             and

                               X 
                            :  
                               
                                  1.

                                        1

                                        P

                                        P

                                        A

                                           A

                                        = 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        + 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           B 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     . 
                               
                             An orthoprobability function  
                               P 
                              is said to be  positive  if and only if for all  
                               A 
                             and  
                               B 
                             in  
                               X

                               P

                                           X 
                                           )

                                        1

                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           ∅

                                        0

                                        0 
                                        ≤ 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A

Ortho finite additivity : If  
                                        A 
                                        ⊆ 
                                        
                                           
                                              B 
                                           
                                           
                                              ⊥ 
                                           
                                        
                                     , then  
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ∪ 
                                           B

                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        + 
                                        P 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           B 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     .

For a boolean algebra of events  
                         B 
                      ,  
                         P 
                        is an orthoprobability function on  
                         B 
                        if and only if it is a boolean probability function on  
                         B 
                      . As an example of how orthoprobability can differ from boolean probability, consider the case where

The positivity of an orthoprobability function eliminates the existence of nonempty events of probability 0. In the development of the theory presented here, it is an important property for non-boolean ortholattices. For the boolean case, it is not needed for the range of topics covered in this article. It is used in the proof of  Theorem 21  below and its applications.  Theorem 21  shows that each ortholattice having a positive orthoprobability function is orthomodular. A different method for dealings with nonempty events of probability 0 is to define probabilistic concepts on an extension of the real number system that includes positive infinitesimal numbers. This approach is taken in  Narens (2015) .

Orthomodular lattice algebras have a rich algebraic structure that makes some of them interesting candidates for logics and domains of probability functions. This section presents some basic facts about them that are useful in the development of probability theories.

Many concepts in orthomodular lattice theory are algebraic abstractions from concepts of Hilbert spaces. One of the most important is “orthogonality”, which is abstracted to the following for orthomodular lattice algebras:

Definition 13 
                         Let  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  
                                     
                                        , 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⊥ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             be an orthomodular lattice algebra of events and  
                               A 
                             and  
                               B 
                             be in  
                               X 
                            . Then  
                               A 
                             is said to be  orthogonal  to  
                               B 
                            , in symbols,  
                               A 
                               ⊥ 
                               B 
                            , if and only if  
                               B 
                               ⊆ 
                               
                                  
                                     A 
                                  
                                  
                                     ⊥ 
                                  
                               
                            .  □

The important orthoprobabilistic properties about orthogonal events are that they are disjoint and their orthoprobabilities add:

Theorem 18 
                         
                            Suppose  
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒

                                  〉 
                               
                             
                            is an orthomodular lattice algebra of events,  
                            
                               A 
                             
                            and  
                            
                               B 
                             
                            in  
                            
                               X 
                                are such that  
                            
                               A 
                               ⊥ 
                               B 
                            
                            , and  
                            
                               P 
                                is an orthoprobability function on  
                            
                               X 
                            
                            . Then 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        A 
                                        ⋓ 
                                        B 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     P 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        A 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     + 
                                     P 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        B 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     .

Proof 
                         Theorems 2.27 and 2.28 of  Narens (2015) .  □

Theorems 2.27 and 2.28 of  Narens (2015) .  □

In a finite boolean lattice algebra of events, each set-theoretic union of atoms produces an event in the algebra, and if the algebra has three or more atoms, then the orthocomplement of an atom is not an atom. The situation is different for a finite, non-boolean, orthomodular lattice algebra of events: Not every set-theoretic union of atoms is in the algebra, and some algebras with three or more atoms have an atom that has a complement that is also an atom.

Many facts about orthomodular lattice algebras of events are abstractions of algebraic relationships observed in Hilbert spaces and quantum mechanics. This is the case for commutativity of projection operators in Hilbert space. The following definition generalizes a key algebraic property of this type of commutativity to orthomodular lattice algebras. Note that in the definition below, the generalization is stated for ortholattices. For orthomodular lattice algebras of events, it matches the algebraic meaning of “commute” (Statement 5 of  Theorem 20  below).

Definition 14 
                         Let  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  
                                     
                                        , 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⊥ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             be an ortholattice and  
                               A 
                             and  
                               B 
                             be arbitrary elements of  
                               X 
                            . Then  
                               A 
                             is said to  commute  with  
                               B 
                            , in symbols  
                               C 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                            , if and only if  
                               A 
                               = 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  B 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ⋓ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  
                                     
                                        B 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⊥ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                            .  □

                               X

                                  , 
                                  X

                                  ∅

                               A

                                  B

                                  A

In  Definition 11  the concept of “sublattice” was defined. The following special case of “sublattice” plays a critical role in the proof of  Theorem 20  below.

Definition 15 
                         Let  
                               X 
                               =

                                  X

                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  
                                     
                                        ,

                         Y 
                        and sublattice of  
                         X 
                        is that the subalgebra  
                         Y

                         ⊆

                         X

                         X

                         ⊆ 
                      -smallest element of  
                         X 
                      ,  
                         ∅ 
                      , whereas a sublattice of  
                         X 
                        does not necessarily contain these elements.

The following theorem of  Kalmbach (1983)  summarizes five different ways of saying “orthomodular”.

Theorem 20 
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  
                                     
                                        ,

                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             
                            be an ortholattice. Then the following five statements are equivalent. 
                            
                               
                                  1. 
                                  
                                     
                                        X 
                                         is orthomodular.

                                     For all

                                        B

                                              A 
                                           
                                           
                                              ⊥

                                        ∅ 
                                     
                                     , then  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        = 
                                        B 
                                     
                                     .

                                              O 
                                           
                                           
                                              6

The following theorem of  Narens (2015)  is used in the following subsection to relate experimental psychological paradigms to orthomodular lattice algebras.

Theorem 21 
                         
                            Suppose  
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  
                                     
                                        , 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⊥ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             
                            is an ortholattice and  
                            
                               P 
                                is a positive orthoprobability function on  
                            
                               X 
                            
                            . Then  
                            
                               X 
                                is orthomodular.

Suppose  
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  〈 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋓ 
                                  , 
                                  ⋒ 
                                  
                                     
                                        , 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⊥ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ∅ 
                                  〉 
                               
                             
                            is an ortholattice and  
                            
                               P 
                                is a positive orthoprobability function on  
                            
                               X 
                            
                            . Then  
                            
                               X 
                                is orthomodular.

Proof 
                         If  
                               X 
                              has no  
                               
                                  
                                     O 
                                  
                                  
                                     6 
                                  
                               
                             subalgebra, then the theorem follows from Statements 1 and 3 of  Theorem 20 . Suppose  
                               X 
                              had the  
                               
                                  
                                     O 
                                  
                                  
                                     6 
                                  
                               
                             subalgebra shown in  Fig. 5 . A contradiction will be shown. Then for  
                               A 
                             and  
                               B 
                             in  Fig. 5 ,  
                               
                                  
                                     A 
                                     ⋓ 
                                     
                                        
                                           A 
                                        
                                        
                                           ⊥ 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     A 
                                     ⋓ 
                                     
                                        
                                           B 
                                        
                                        
                                           ⊥ 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     X 
                                     .

                               P 
                              is an orthoprobability function,

                                     P 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        X 
                                        )

                               P 
                              is positive and

                                     A

                                              B 
                                           
                                           
                                              ⊥

                                     P 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              A

If  
                               X 
                              has no  
                               
                                  
                                     O 
                                  
                                  
                                     6 
                                  
                               
                             subalgebra, then the theorem follows from Statements 1 and 3 of  Theorem 20 . Suppose  
                               X 
                              had the  
                               
                                  
                                     O 
                                  
                                  
                                     6 
                                  
                               
                             subalgebra shown in  Fig. 5 . A contradiction will be shown. Then for  
                               A

                                           B 
                                        
                                        
                                           ⊥

                                     X 
                                     .

                              is an orthoprobability function,

                                     B

                                     A 
                                  
                                  
                                     ⊥ 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  (23) 
                                  
                                     P 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              B 
                                           
                                           
                                              ⊥ 
                                           
                                        
                                        )

                                     P 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                           
                                              ⊥ 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     . 
                                  
                               
                             Because Eqs.  (22) and (23) , contradict one another, a contradiction has been shown.  □

Greechie (1971)  provided examples of finite orthomodular algebras of events that had no orthoprobability functions. This shows that the hypothesis that  
                         P 
                        is an orthoprobability function restricts the class of orthomodular lattices. A consequence of this restriction is exploited in the next subsection.

Modeling methods inspired by quantum mechanics have recently entered into cognitive psychology and decision theory (e.g.,   Busemeyer & Bruza, 2012 ). They are based on Hilbert space probabilistic modeling, which employs closed subspaces as events. This form of modeling is useful for many of the context and order effects routinely seen in cognitive psychology experiments. These effects often produce empirically observed failures in the finite additivity of probabilities across disjoint events.

This subsection describes a new probability theory due to  Narens (2016b)  that shares many fundamental algebraic characteristics with Hilbert space probability theory. The new probability theory is formulated in terms of how psychological experiments are linked through counterfactual reasoning.

For this article, only a very simplified experimental psychological situation is considered. It is a between-subject paradigm having two experiments, where each experiment involves having subjects making a choice among three alternatives. The ideas and methods involved for this situation extend to situations with more than two experiments as well as other numbers of alternatives and other kinds of experimental paradigms. The subsection’s goal is to show how orthomodular lattice algebras naturally arise from experimental considerations.

A  paradigm  consists of two  experiments ,  
                            
                               
                                  E 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  E 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         . In  
                            
                               
                                  E 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          the subject is given instructions and asked to select one of the three  outcomes 
                         
                            a 
                            , 
                            b 
                            , 
                            c 
                          according to her understanding of the instructions. In  
                            
                               
                                  E 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                          the subject is given different instructions and is asked to select one of the three outcomes  
                            d 
                            , 
                            e 
                            , 
                            f 
                          according to her understanding of the instructions. This paradigm is denoted by the symbol  
                            P 
                         , and it is a  between subject paradigm , that is, each subject participates in only one of  
                            P 
                         ’s experiments. The outcomes  
                            a 
                            , 
                            b 
                            , 
                            c 
                            , 
                            d 
                            , 
                            e 
                            , 
                            f 
                          are considered distinct. This is even so if they are identical physically. There will be a way described later that allows for identification of outcomes across experiments, effectively treating them as the same outcome.

                            d 
                         , because no subject participated in both experiments. Statement 3 cannot be checked empirically.

In order to illustrate the main ideas involved in the construction of an event space of “propositions” described below, it will be assumed that  
                            P 
                         ’s theory consists of Theory 1 above. This will allow  
                            P 
                          to be identified with a well-known quantum situation while simultaneously being a description of a psychological paradigm. Most of the concepts used in the below construction of an event space of “propositions” for paradigm  
                            P 
                          are very general and extend to other paradigms and theories.

Proof 
                            Theorem 22 of  Narens (2016b) .  □

Theorem 22 of  Narens (2016b) .  □

Lattices appear in physics that are lattice isomorphic to  
                            P 
                           and based on experimental paradigms similar to  
                            P 
                         . An example of such a lattice is described in  Fig. 6 
                         .

It has been noted in the literature (e.g.,   Busemeyer & Bruza, 2012 ) that there are many experimental designs in psychology that are structurally similar to those in quantum mechanics. Various researchers have taken advantage of this, and used methods of quantum mechanics to model psychological phenomena. These quantum-like models give different insights into cognitive processing, and provide equal or better fit to experimental psychological data ( Busemeyer & Bruza, 2012 ).

A criticism of this kind of “quantum mechanical” modeling is that its foundational principles are not adequately justified. This criticism applies to quantum mechanics as well as psychology. For quantum mechanics, alternatives have been suggested, but for various reasons they do not function as well as the standard theory. For psychology, much of fundamental symmetry inherent in quantum phenomena is obscure. This is not surprising, because the foundation of quantum mechanics was designed to model the kinds of symmetry found in quantum phenomena. An exception appears to be the use of closed subspaces of a Hilbert space as events. While being a part of the foundation of quantum mechanics, it applies to many other phenomena. It appears to me to provide a simple and useful way to model dynamic aspects of cognitive phenomena.

The psychological paradigm modeling of this subsection has many commonalities with the modeling of events in a Hilbert space, in particular, both yield probabilistic event spaces that are orthomodular lattice algebras. However, in other aspects they are very different. Some of the differences are discussed in  Narens (2016b) .

Three kinds of non-boolean event algebras–pseudo complemented and distributive, Hilbert space, and orthomodular–have recently appeared in the literature for modeling probabilistic cognitive phenomena. They are all generalizations of boolean probability theory. They all appear to be useful in providing alternative explanations for unintuitive experimental findings in the cognitive literature as well providing insights into possible cognitive mechanisms. All three provide richer mathematical event spaces and probabilistic concepts than boolean probability theory. At this time, they have been mainly used in psychology to explain phenomena where boolean explanations appear inadequate. The three new probability theories, which are very different from one another, often can be used to explain the same troublesome phenomena. At this time, experimental research has not been designed to distinguish them empirically.
